segments.caption: The video begins with a black screen displaying a warning in white text: 'This film contains images which some viewers may find distressing,' setting a somber and cautionary tone. The screen then transitions to a dark, almost entirely black background, where a disturbing and grotesque painting gradually emerges from the right side of the frame. The painting, identified by on-screen text as 'Saturn Devouring His Son' by Francisco Goya (1819–1823), depicts a monstrous, emaciated figure with wild, disheveled hair and wide, staring eyes, violently consuming a small, pale human form—presumably his son—whose limbs are visible and bloodied. The camera slowly zooms in on the painting, focusing increasingly on the figure’s face and the act of devouring, emphasizing the raw, chaotic brushwork and the visceral horror of the scene. The lighting remains dim, highlighting only the central figures against the deep blackness, enhancing the painting’s nightmarish and unsettling atmosphere. As the zoom continues, the viewer is drawn into the grotesque details: the blood-streaked mouth, the hollow eyes, and the contorted expression of madness or desperation. The painting’s dark, turbulent textures and the stark contrast between the flesh tones and the black background create a deeply disturbing visual experience, culminating in an extreme close-up of the devouring figure’s face, frozen in a moment of primal, horrifying intensity.

discovered_new_entities.Saturn Devouring His Son Painting: A dark, chaotic oil painting by Francisco Goya depicting a monstrous, gaunt figure with wild hair and wide eyes devouring a small, bloodied human form; characterized by violent brushwork, stark contrasts, and a deeply unsettling atmosphere.

segments.caption: The video opens with a rapid montage of iconic artworks, including a close-up of Hokusai’s *The Great Wave off Kanagawa*, Warhol’s *Marilyn Monroe* silkscreen, da Vinci’s *Mona Lisa*, and Van Goya’s *The Two Fridas*, before transitioning to Van Gogh’s *The Starry Night* with the title 'GREAT ART EXPLAINED' overlaid. The focus then shifts to Francisco Goya’s *The Black Paintings*, beginning with *Witches’ Sabbath* (1819–1823), a dark, chaotic scene filled with grotesque figures and a central demonic figure, rendered in thick, expressive brushstrokes. This is followed by *Two Old Men Eating Soup* (1819–1823), depicting two emaciated, almost skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, their faces contorted with hunger and despair. A contrast is drawn with *The Family of the Infante Don Luis* (1783), a brightly lit, formal portrait of an aristocratic family in rich attire, suggesting Goya’s earlier, more polished style. The sequence returns to the dark tone with *Women Laughing* (1819–1823), showing two women with unsettling, wide grins, their features distorted and eerie. Next, *A Pilgrimage to San Isidro* (1819–1823) appears, a crowded, frenzied scene of people in a religious procession, their faces contorted in ecstasy or madness. The video then transitions to a real-world museum setting, where visitors walk through a dimly lit gallery, observing the Black Paintings displayed on the walls under focused lighting, emphasizing their somber, haunting presence. The atmosphere shifts from the historical and artistic to the contemporary, grounding the viewer in the physical space where these works are experienced today.

segments.caption: The video opens in a dimly lit Museum Gallery, where large, dark paintings from Goya’s Black Paintings series are displayed under focused lighting, creating a somber and contemplative atmosphere. Visitors move quietly through the space, observing the works. The camera then zooms in on the painting 'Altropos (The Fates)' by Francisco Goya (1819–1823), revealing three grotesque, dark figures in a desolate landscape, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork. The scene transitions to a close-up of a distorted, screaming face from another dark painting, emphasizing Goya’s later, more nightmarish style. The video then shifts to a blurred image of a woman in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, identified as 'Self-Portrait at an Easel' (1790), suggesting a transition from Goya’s later darkness to his earlier, more vibrant works. An aerial shot of a historic bullring in Madrid, marked with the year 1775, provides geographical and temporal context. The video then presents a series of Goya’s earlier paintings: a close-up of 'Majo With Guitar (Detail)' (1779), showing a man in a tricorn hat with a contemplative expression; 'Boys Playing at Being Soldiers' (1777), depicting children in military costumes in a playful, pastoral setting; and 'Charles IV of Spain and his Family (Detail)' (1800), where Goya’s self-portrait is subtly embedded in the background, observing the royal family. The sequence continues with 'Charles III in Hunting Dress' (1786), showing the king in a gray coat and tricorn hat, holding a rifle, and concludes with 'Charles IV in Red' (1789), a formal portrait of the king in a red robe with a blue sash and medals, set against a dark, dramatic background. The narrative traces Goya’s artistic evolution from light and playful to dark and introspective, using both his paintings and contextual imagery to illustrate this journey.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a dynamic visual journey through Francisco Goya’s artistic evolution, beginning with formal royal portraits and transitioning into darker, more introspective and chaotic works. It opens with a close-up of 'Fernando VII' (1814), a regal portrait showing the king in a white uniform with a gold-embellished sash and a high white cravat, set against a dark background. This is followed by 'Joseph Bonaparte' (1808), a full-length portrait of the French king in opulent white and gold regalia, seated on a throne with a golden drape behind him, exuding imperial authority. The sequence then shifts to Goya’s self-portraits: first, a youthful 'Self-Portrait' (1771) with dark hair and a somber expression, then a later, more somber 'Self-Portrait with Doctor Arrieta' (1820), where Goya appears frail and ill, supported by a doctor holding a glass, reflecting his physical decline. The tone darkens further with 'Two Old Men' (1819–1823), a haunting depiction of two emaciated, skeletal figures sharing a meager meal, rendered in thick, expressive brushstrokes. The narrative intensifies with 'The Second of May 1808' (1814), a chaotic battle scene filled with horses, soldiers, and civilians in violent struggle, capturing the horror of war. This is intercut with 'The Marquise de la Solana' (1793), a delicate portrait of a woman in a white shawl with a pink flower in her hair, providing a brief contrast. The segment then transitions to Goya’s etchings: 'And They are Like Wild Beasts' (published 1863), a stark black-and-white print depicting violent, animalistic figures, and 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters' (1799), showing a sleeping man surrounded by owls and bats, symbolizing the dangers of ignorance. The final image, 'A Way of Flying' (published 1864), shows a grotesque figure with wings and a long tongue, flying over a landscape, reinforcing Goya’s descent into the surreal and nightmarish. The sequence traces Goya’s artistic arc from courtly elegance to profound psychological and social critique, marked by increasing darkness and emotional intensity.

segments.caption: The video begins with a dark, etched image titled 'A Way of Flying' by Francisco Goya, published in 1864, depicting a grotesque winged figure with a long tongue soaring over a landscape, accompanied by smaller flying creatures. This transitions to a self-portrait of Goya from 1799, showing him in profile wearing a tall hat, with the text 'Franco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' beneath. The scene then shifts to a series of Goya’s etchings from 'Los Caprichos,' including a man being attacked by an owl and a man in a long robe holding a bird, all displayed alongside the self-portrait. A close-up of 'A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!' (Plate 39), published in 1863, reveals a muscular figure in a horned helmet, seemingly dragging a corpse. The video then transitions to a painted self-portrait of Goya from 1795, showing him with dark curly hair and a colorful cravat, holding a palette. This is followed by a dramatic painting of 'Napoleon Accepts the Surrender of Madrid' (1810) by Antoine-Jean Gros, depicting Napoleon in military regalia receiving surrender from Spanish officials. Next, 'Coronation of Napoleon' (1804) by Jacques Louis-David shows Napoleon placing a crown on his own head, surrounded by courtiers. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of the Royal Palace of Madrid, a grand neoclassical building with a green dome. A portrait of Nicolas Guye, a French Army officer, painted by Goya in 1810 under French occupation, appears, showing him in ornate military uniform with medals. The video then shows 'Napoleon on the Outskirts of Madrid' (1812) by Carle Verner, depicting Napoleon in a tense meeting with Spanish figures amidst smoke and chaos. Finally, a formal portrait of Napoleon by Paul Delaroche (1838) is shown, with Napoleon in a dark uniform against a red background, exuding authority and solemnity. The sequence moves from Goya’s dark, satirical etchings to historical paintings of Napoleon and Spanish royalty, reflecting the political turmoil of the era and Goya’s role as both a critic and court painter.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a formal portrait of Napoleon by Paul Delaroche (1838), depicting him in a dark military uniform with gold epaulettes and medals, seated against a deep red background, exuding authority and solemnity. The scene transitions to a satirical political cartoon, 'The Disappointed King of Spain' by Isaac Cruikshank (1808), showing a comically exaggerated King Charles IV in royal attire, seated on a broken chair with arms raised in despair, as his crown flies away labeled 'Sincerity,' while a figure resembling Napoleon mocks him with a speech bubble. Next, the video shifts to a classical allegorical engraving, 'Truth, Light, Philosophy and Reason' by Benoît-Luis Prévost (1764), portraying a group of robed figures in a mythological setting, with one holding a torch and another a laurel wreath, symbolizing enlightenment ideals. This is followed by a dark, dramatic painting, 'Condemned by The Inquisition' by Eugenio Lucas Velázquez (1860), depicting a nightmarish scene of torture and persecution, with figures in conical hats and chains, illuminated by flickering torchlight. The sequence continues with Francisco Goya’s etching 'And There’s Nothing to be Done (Plate 15)' (published 1864), a stark black-and-white image of a man standing over a fallen figure, evoking despair and helplessness. Then, Goya’s 'Attack of a Woman' (1811) appears, a dark, violent oil painting showing a woman being assaulted by a monstrous figure, with another figure in the background holding a torch. This is followed by 'Simpleton’s Folly' (1799), a grotesque etching of a man in a state of madness, clutching his head. The video then shows 'Guerrilla Warfare' by Roque Gameiro (1917), a colorful, dynamic scene of Spanish guerrillas ambushing French troops in a rocky landscape. The next image is Goya’s 'The Third of May 1808' (1814), a powerful depiction of a man with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies, under a dark sky. The segment concludes with 'Landing of Ferdinand VII in El Puerto' by José Aparicio (1823), a grand, celebratory painting of the king’s return, with nobles, soldiers, and civilians gathered on a ship, under a bright sky, symbolizing restoration and hope.

segments.caption: The video begins with a series of classical portraits, starting with 'Charles III as a Boy' (Jean Ranc, 1724), depicting a young boy with curly blonde hair in an ornate blue and gold robe, holding a small flower and resting his hand on an open book adorned with flowers. This transitions to 'Charles III' (Anton Raphael Mengs, 1765), showing the same figure as an adult in armor with a red and gold sash, seated with a dignified expression. Next, a detail from 'The Family of the Infante Don Luis' (Francisco Goya, 1784) appears, showing a woman in a red dress holding a child, with another figure in dark attire beside them, rendered in warm, rich tones. The sequence continues with 'Newton' (William Blake, 1804), a mythic depiction of a nude, muscular man with curly hair, draped in white cloth, intently drawing geometric figures on a stone tablet amidst a rocky, natural landscape. This is followed by 'A Philosopher Lecturing on the Orrery' (Joseph Wright of Derby, 1766), a dramatic scene with a central figure in a red coat demonstrating a glowing orrery to an attentive audience in a dimly lit room, with children peering through the apparatus. Then, 'Thomas Jefferson' (Rembrandt Peale, 1800) is shown, a solemn portrait of the statesman with white hair and a dark coat, gazing directly at the viewer. The next image is 'Ferdinand VII' (Francisco Goya, 1814), a formal portrait of the king in a red vest and black coat with a sash, seated beside a crown. This is followed by 'The Inquisition Tribunal' (Francisco Goya, 1812), a dark, crowded scene of a religious trial with figures in conical hats and robes, illuminated by a single light source, evoking tension and oppression. The video then shifts to grainy black-and-white archival footage of a chaotic outdoor scene, possibly a historical conflict or protest, with figures running and falling amidst debris. Finally, the segment ends with a stark black-and-white etching titled '2. Dark Goya (1792 onwards)', showing a violent, grotesque scene of figures in struggle, rendered in dense cross-hatching, with a man being attacked by others in a dark, confined space, emphasizing Goya’s later, darker artistic phase.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a curated sequence of Francisco Goya’s artworks, transitioning from dark, etched prints to oil paintings, showcasing the evolution of his style and thematic depth. It begins with a close-up of an etching titled 'No se puede mirar' (It Cannot Be Looked At), depicting a grotesque, contorted figure in agony, rendered in stark black-and-white with heavy cross-hatching. This is followed by 'This is Worse' (1864), another etching showing a man in despair clutching his head, with a figure behind him holding a knife, emphasizing psychological torment. The sequence shifts to 'Two Old Men' (1819–1823), a somber oil painting of two emaciated figures sharing a meager meal, their faces gaunt and expressive, painted in thick, textured strokes. Next, 'The Parasol' (1777) appears—a bright, pastoral scene with a woman in a blue and yellow dress holding a green parasol, accompanied by a man in a brown jacket, set against a serene landscape. The mood darkens again with 'Witches’ Sabbath' (1819–1823), a chaotic, nightmarish oil painting of grotesque figures surrounding a demonic goat-like creature, rendered in dark, swirling brushwork. The segment continues with 'They Do Not Want To' (1864), an etching of a man being attacked while another figure raises a knife, echoing themes of violence and helplessness. A brief, blurred transition leads to 'The Game of Horse and Rider' (1792), a cheerful painting of children playing, one riding on another’s shoulders, set against a light, airy background. The final and most extended scene is 'Yard With Lunatics (Corral de Locos)' (1794), a dark, haunting oil painting depicting a group of agitated, half-naked figures in a dimly lit, confined space, some embracing, others screaming or gesturing wildly, with a single light source from a barred window illuminating the chaos. The painting’s oppressive atmosphere and raw emotional intensity dominate the segment’s conclusion.

The final frames focus on the hands continuing to work on the plate, emphasizing the precision and labor involved in creating such etchings.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a close-up of hands meticulously engraving fine lines into a metal plate using a sharp etching tool, illustrating the labor-intensive process of intaglio printmaking. The scene transitions to a split-screen comparison, showing the engraving tool in action on the left and the resulting detailed, high-contrast etching on the right, with text overlay reading 'Engraving directly on to metal.' This is followed by another split-screen showing 'Engraving on soft ground,' with a dynamic, expressive etching of a figure raising arms amidst smaller figures, suggesting a religious or mythological scene. The video then presents side-by-side comparisons of historical prints and their corresponding copper plates, including 'Ecce Homo' by Jan Norblin (1770s) and a self-portrait etching by Goya, labeled 'Original' and 'Copper Plate,' highlighting the fidelity of the print to the engraved surface. The process continues with a hand submerging a metal plate into a steaming acid bath, followed by scrubbing the plate with a brush to reveal the etched image beneath the resist. The final frames show a close-up comparison of an original etching and its copper plate, emphasizing the intricate lines and textures preserved in the metal, with the plate’s surface reflecting light to reveal the depth and precision of the engraved lines.

segments.caption: The video begins in a dimly lit, shadowy environment, possibly a cellar or workshop, where a hand uses a torch to illuminate a wooden surface, revealing a textured, layered object that appears to be a stack of aged, possibly damp, wooden planks or boards. The torchlight moves across the surface, highlighting cracks and grain. The scene transitions to a close-up of a hand lifting a framed, sepia-toned etching from a dark surface. The etching, titled 'Three Stages of Aquatint' by Julius Caesar Ibbetson (1794), depicts a pastoral scene with a donkey carrying a family, a child leading it, and other figures in the background, rendered in fine, expressive lines. The hand then holds the print up to the light, examining it closely. The scene shifts to a modern, well-lit studio where a person, identified by on-screen text as 'Roisin', is preparing materials for printmaking. She grinds a yellowish substance, labeled 'Roisin', in a white mortar with a pestle. She then applies the ground to a copper plate using a brush, and the plate is submerged into a steaming acid bath, where it is gently agitated. The final shot is a close-up of the etched plate’s surface, revealing a fine, granular texture created by the acid etching process, indicating the successful creation of tonal areas for aquatint printing.

segments.caption: The video begins with a side-by-side comparison of an etching before and after the application of aquatint, highlighting the transformation from a stark line drawing to a tonal, textured image with added depth and shading. This is followed by a close-up of a detailed etching depicting a donkey carrying a family, with a child leading it, illustrating the use of aquatint for creating atmospheric effects. The scene transitions to a full view of Francisco Goya’s etching 'Can’t Anyone Unleash Us? (Plate 75)' from 1799, showing a woman struggling under the weight of a large owl, symbolizing oppression or ignorance. The video then shifts to a historical newspaper, 'Diario de Madrid,' dated December 16, 1836, with a quote overlay stating, 'In none of the compositions that form this collection has the author proposed to ridicule the particular faults of one or another individual,' emphasizing Goya’s broader social critique. This is followed by a grid of Goya’s 'Los Caprichos' etchings, showcasing a range of satirical and grotesque scenes, including 'Bon Voyage (Plate 64),' which depicts a chaotic, nightmarish scene of figures in distress, rendered with dense cross-hatching and dramatic shading. The final frames focus on a close-up of 'Bon Voyage,' highlighting the expressive, agitated faces and the dark, oppressive atmosphere of the etching.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of Francisco Goya’s etching *Here Comes the Bogeyman (Plate 3)*, 1799, depicting a monstrous, shadowy figure looming over a terrified child, rendered in stark black-and-white with heavy cross-hatching and dramatic contrast. The scene transitions to *Self-Portrait (Plate 1)*, 1799, a profile view of Goya in a tall black hat and formal coat, with the inscription 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' beneath, set against a plain background. Next, the video shows *Charles IV and Family*, 1800, a grand, colorful portrait of the Spanish royal family in opulent attire, with Goya’s self-portrait subtly visible in the background. This is followed by *Battle of Cape Vincent (1797)*, 1800, by Robert Cleveley, a dramatic naval scene with ships engaged in combat under a stormy sky. The sequence continues with a chaotic, colorful print of a crowd in 18th-century clothing, possibly a political or social upheaval, followed by a satirical cartoon depicting King Charles IV and his family in exaggerated, infantile forms, labeled 'Prince of Asturias' and 'Imperial Grandee'. The video then returns to Goya’s *The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43)*, 1799, showing a man asleep at a desk, surrounded by owls and bats, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible. Finally, the segment ends with *The French Revolution*, 1792, by Léon Cogniet, a large, dynamic painting of a revolutionary crowd with soldiers, horses, and smoke, capturing the chaos and fervor of the event. The sequence moves from Goya’s dark, satirical etchings to historical paintings and political cartoons, highlighting themes of power, reason, and societal upheaval.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a classical portrait of Jean-Jacques Rousseau, painted by François Guerin in 1760, showing him with powdered white hair, a calm expression, and formal 18th-century attire. Overlaid text gradually reveals his famous quote: 'Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains.' The scene transitions to a historical book titled 'Philosophie' from 1793, featuring an engraved frontispiece depicting a philosopher at a desk, surrounded by symbolic figures and books, with the word 'PHILOSOPHY' prominently displayed. The video then shifts to a detailed etching titled 'The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters' (Plate 43) by Francisco Goya, showing a man asleep at a desk, surrounded by owls, bats, and a cat, with the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible. The camera zooms in on various elements of the etching—first the owls and bats, then the cat, and finally the sleeping man’s face and hands—emphasizing the intricate linework and dark, ominous atmosphere. The scene concludes with a full view of the etching, followed by a split-screen showing the etching on the right and a profile self-portrait of Goya on the left, labeled 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor,' linking the artwork to its creator. The overall atmosphere evolves from intellectual reflection to a dark, symbolic warning about the dangers of ignorance and reason’s dormancy.

segments.caption: The video begins with a split-screen showing the Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Caprichos) on the left and The Sleep of Reason Produces Monsters (Plate 43) Etching on the right, both rendered in fine etching lines with the latter featuring owls, bats, and a cat surrounding a sleeping man, and the Spanish inscription 'El sueño de la razón produce monstruos' visible. The scene transitions to a close-up of The Bewitched Man painting (1798), depicting a man in a dark cloak recoiling in terror from a monstrous, horned figure in a dark, starry night. This is followed by Out Hunting for Teeth (1799), an etching showing a man pulling teeth from another’s mouth, with a fiery scene of a burning pyre and silhouetted figures in the background, evoking a sense of persecution. The video then returns to the Self-Portrait (Frontispiece for Los Caprichos), zooming in on Goya’s profile in his tall black hat. A rapid sequence follows, showcasing several etchings from Los Caprichos: A Man with a Winged Monster (Plate 54), A Girl with a Sword (Plate 56), A Frightened Man (Plate 81), and And His House is on Fire (Plate 18), each depicting grotesque, satirical, or violent scenes with expressive lines and dramatic shading. The final etching shown is No one has seen us (Plate 79), depicting three men drinking from mugs beside a barrel, with the handwritten Spanish text 'Nadie nos ha visto' circled at the bottom, emphasizing secrecy and complicity. The overall atmosphere is dark, satirical, and critical, moving from Goya’s self-representation to his scathing social commentary through etchings, punctuated by a brief, fiery historical scene suggesting inquisition or persecution.

segments.caption: The video begins with a dark, red-tinted close-up of a man’s face from the 1922 film *Haxan*, directed by Benjamin Christensen, his expression contorted in anguish or ecstasy, setting a tone of psychological horror. The scene transitions to a close-up of hands bound with rope, suggesting torture or ritual, before shifting to a series of Francisco Goya’s dark, haunting paintings. First, *Men Reading (From the Black Paintings)* (1819–1823) is shown, depicting a group of men huddled around a single light source, their faces grotesque and shadowed, evoking despair and isolation. This is followed by *Night Scene From the Inquisition* (1810), a chaotic, fiery scene of persecution under a blazing sky, with figures in torment. Then, *The Inquisition Tribunal* (1819) appears, a dimly lit courtroom filled with figures in conical hats, illuminated by a single candle, emphasizing oppression and judgment. The sequence continues with a split-screen of Goya’s etchings: a profile self-portrait labeled 'Francisco Goya y Lucientes, Pintor' on the left, and a monstrous, hunched figure on the right, possibly from *The Disparate General* or *A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!*, both rendered in stark, expressive lines. The video then cuts to a modern museum gallery, the San Fernando Fine Art Royal Academy, where a large wooden printing press stands in the center, surrounded by framed etchings on the walls, suggesting a connection between Goya’s techniques and their preservation. This is followed by a black-and-white film clip from *Reign of Terror* (1949, directed by Anthony Mann), showing a frenzied crowd in revolutionary fervor, raising their arms and weapons. The final image is a regal portrait of Napoleon on his imperial throne by Jean Auguste Dominique Ingres (1806), with the emperor in full ceremonial attire, holding a scepter and orb, his face stern and authoritative, framed by a golden halo, contrasting the earlier chaos with imperial power.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a regal portrait, likely of Joseph Bonaparte, seated on a golden throne, adorned in opulent white and gold royal attire with a red cloak, holding a scepter and orb, exuding authority. The scene transitions to a chaotic battle painting, 'The Second of May 1808' by Francisco Goya (1814), depicting a frenzied clash between Spanish civilians and French soldiers, with horses rearing and figures in violent struggle, rendered in earthy tones and dynamic motion. The next frame shows 'The Third of May 1808' (El Tres de Mayo 1808), a powerful night scene by Goya (1814), where a man in a white shirt raises his arms before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance. This is followed by grainy black-and-white archival footage of soldiers in a similar execution scene, reinforcing the painting’s historical weight. The video then presents Goya’s 'Self-Portrait' (1815), a somber, close-up of the artist with dark, curly hair and a weary expression, set against a dark background. Next, 'The French General Guye' (1810) appears, a formal portrait of a French officer in a richly decorated uniform with gold epaulettes and a red sash, symbolizing military authority. The sequence shifts to Goya’s etchings: 'Bury Them and Keep Quiet' (published 1864), a stark black-and-white image of a man standing over a pile of naked, lifeless bodies, followed by 'What is This Hubbub?' (1864), showing a man in distress clutching his head. Then, 'For a Clasp Knife' (1864) depicts a man seated on a pile of heads, holding a knife, with a crowd below. The final frame is a split-screen: the left side shows 'The Third of May 1808' again, while the right side displays 'With or Without Reason' (1819–1823), an etching of a woman holding a child as a soldier attacks, emphasizing violence and helplessness. The narrative flows from royal power to violent conflict, personal reflection, military authority, and finally to Goya’s dark, satirical etchings, creating a progression from historical events to psychological and moral critique.

segments.caption: The video begins with a full view of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, a powerful and emotionally charged painting depicting a Spanish civilian being executed by a French firing squad under a dark, moonlit sky. The central figure, a man in a white shirt and yellow trousers, stands with arms raised in a gesture of surrender or appeal, surrounded by other victims and soldiers. The camera slowly zooms in on the central figure, emphasizing his anguished expression and outstretched hands, while the surrounding figures—some pleading, others already fallen—add to the scene’s horror. The lighting is dramatic, with stark contrasts between light and shadow, highlighting the tension and tragedy. The camera then pans slightly to show the firing squad, their rifles aimed directly at the victim, reinforcing the inevitability of death. Midway through, the video transitions to a split-screen comparison: the left side continues to show the Goya painting, while the right side displays Pablo Picasso’s *Guernica* (1937), drawing a visual and thematic parallel between the two works of war and suffering. The split-screen highlights the shared motif of raised arms and distorted figures, underscoring the enduring power of the image. The video then cuts to Caravaggio’s *The Calling of Saint Matthew* (1599), showing a dramatic moment of divine intervention with a beam of light illuminating the figures in a dark room, creating a stark contrast in both subject and style. Finally, the video returns to *The Third of May 1808*, with the camera pulling back to the full composition, reinforcing its monumental scale and emotional weight. Throughout, the atmosphere remains somber and intense, with the dark, night-time setting and the violent subject matter creating a profound sense of historical tragedy and human suffering.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dramatic, full-frame view of Francisco Goya’s *The Third of May 1808*, a powerful oil painting depicting a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark, moonlit sky. The scene is tense and emotionally charged, with soldiers in dark uniforms aiming their rifles, and the central figure’s desperate gesture conveying resistance and horror. The camera then transitions to a series of Goya’s etchings from *The Disasters of War*, each shown in close-up with on-screen text identifying the title and publication year (1864). The first etching, *Bury Them and Keep Quiet*, shows a man standing over a pile of naked, lifeless bodies, rendered in stark black-and-white with heavy cross-hatching, evoking silence and complicity. Next, *The Women are Courageous* depicts a woman defending a child from an attacker, her expression fierce and determined. This is followed by *Gloomy Presentiments of What Must Come to Pass*, a haunting image of a gaunt, shirtless man with arms outstretched, his face contorted in dread, set against a dark, textured background. The sequence continues with *Why?*, showing a man being violently attacked by soldiers, his mouth open in a silent scream, and *And it Cannot be Helped*, where a woman mourns over a fallen man, her posture slumped in despair. *I Have Seen This* shows a woman fleeing with a child as a soldier pursues, with the Spanish inscription 'Yo lo vi' circled in red at the bottom, emphasizing witness and memory. The final etching, *What Good is a Cup?*, portrays a woman offering a cup to a dying man, her face sorrowful, while another figure lies lifeless beside them, rendered in dense, expressive lines. The atmosphere throughout is one of profound suffering, moral outrage, and existential dread, with each etching building on the previous to create a cumulative narrative of war’s brutality and human resilience.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a rapid, haunting sequence of Francisco Goya’s etchings from his *The Disasters of War* series, published in 1864, each framed with a title and the artist’s name. The narrative unfolds through a series of grim, expressive black-and-white images depicting human suffering, absurdity, and violence. It begins with *The Same*, showing a man raising a club over a pile of bodies, followed by *Cartloads for the Cemetery*, where figures load corpses onto a cart in a bleak, shadowed setting. The sequence continues with *A Heroic Feat! With Dead Men!*—a monstrous figure dragging a corpse through a rocky landscape—then *The Worst is to Beg*, where emaciated figures huddle around a dead body, one woman in a long dress standing in despair. *Perhaps They are Another Breed* shows skeletal figures interacting with a man in a hat, suggesting dehumanization. *Way to Fly* presents a nightmarish scene of winged, bat-like creatures flying in darkness. *The Rope is Breaking* depicts a man in a robe falling from a rope, surrounded by a crowd of heads, while *Against the Common Good* shows a demonic figure with bat wings attacking a man. The sequence culminates in *They Escape Among the Flames*, where figures flee in terror through a fiery, chaotic landscape. The atmosphere is consistently dark, oppressive, and filled with despair, rendered in dense cross-hatching and stark contrasts, emphasizing the horrors of war and human degradation. On-screen text consistently identifies each etching with its title, artist, and publication year, reinforcing the historical and artistic context.

The final frames show a detail from 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting,' depicting a group of dark, contorted figures in a nightmarish scene, reinforcing the theme of Goya’s late, disturbing works. The atmosphere is consistently dark and oppressive, with a focus on the evolution of Goya’s art from formal portraiture to nightmarish, expressionistic horror.

segments.caption: The video begins with a grid montage of Francisco Goya’s dark, expressive paintings, including the visceral 'Saturn Devouring His Son Painting', the chaotic 'Witches’ Sabbath Painting', and the somber 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', establishing a tone of psychological intensity and social critique. The sequence transitions to a close-up of the 'Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting', emphasizing the emaciated figures and their desperate expressions. This is followed by a detailed zoom into the 'Women Laughing Painting', highlighting the grotesque, distorted grins of the two women, evoking madness or mockery. The scene then shifts to a museum gallery where a group of visitors gathers around a large, dark painting, identified as 'A Pilgrimage to San Isidro Painting', with a guide gesturing toward it. segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of a grotesque, darkly painted face from Francisco Goya’s *Altropos (The Fates)*, emphasizing its distorted features, wide eye, and thick, expressive brushwork in earthy tones against a black background. The camera then pans down to reveal a hand holding a spoon, rendered with the same heavy, textured strokes, suggesting a connection to the *Two Old Men Eating Soup Painting*. The scene transitions to a full view of *Altropos (The Fates)*, showing three dark, emaciated figures in a desolate landscape, each engaged in a grim task: one spins thread, another measures it with a ring, and the third cuts it with shears. The painting is then compared side-by-side with a classical engraving of the same mythological trio—Clotho, Lachesis, and Atropos—highlighting Goya’s grotesque reinterpretation of the traditional, idealized figures. The video zooms in on each figure, contrasting Goya’s dark, monstrous forms with the engraving’s smooth, classical anatomy. A white outline then isolates the central figure, Lachesis, emphasizing her distorted, almost skeletal form and the eerie, unsettling atmosphere of the painting. The overall mood is one of decay, fate, and psychological horror, underscored by the stark contrast between Goya’s dark vision and the Enlightenment-era idealism of the engraving.

segments.caption: The video segment presents a curated sequence of Francisco Goya’s portraits, primarily focusing on women, progressing chronologically and stylistically from his later, more intimate works to earlier, formal pieces. It begins with *The Milkmaid of Bordeaux (Believed to be Leocadia)*, a soft, impressionistic 1827 painting showing a woman in profile with dark hair and a light headscarf, her face gently illuminated against a muted greenish background. This transitions to a sketchy, monochromatic drawing titled *I am Still Learning* (1826), depicting a woman in a white dress kneeling before an old man, rendered in loose charcoal strokes. The next image, *A Woman Kneeling Before an Old Man* (1820), continues the sketchy style, showing a similar scene with more defined figures. The sequence then shifts to *La Leocadia* (1827), a somber, dark painting of a woman in a black dress leaning over a stone ledge, her face illuminated against a pale sky, with a metal railing in the background. This is followed by *Majas on a Balcony* (1812), a richly detailed painting of two women in ornate golden dresses with lace veils, leaning on a black railing, their expressions serene and composed. The final images are *The White Duchess* (1795), a full-length portrait of a woman in a white dress with a red sash and bow, standing in a landscape with a small dog at her feet, and a split-screen comparison with *The Black Duchess* (1797), where the same woman is depicted in a black dress with gold trim, maintaining the same pose and background. The lighting evolves from soft and intimate in the later works to brighter and more formal in the earlier portraits, reflecting Goya’s stylistic range and emotional depth. On-screen text consistently identifies each painting with its title, artist, and year, anchoring the visual narrative in historical context.

segments.caption: The video begins with a close-up of Pieter Claesz’s *Vanitas Still Life* (1630), a somber oil painting featuring a human skull resting on a stack of books, flanked by a glass, a pipe, and a hourglass, all rendered in muted browns and grays under dramatic chiaroscuro lighting, symbolizing mortality and the transience of life. The scene transitions to a grainy black-and-white archival photograph from the front page of the Spanish newspaper *ABC*, dated November 30, 1919, showing a group of men in early 20th-century attire—coats, hats, and one in a white lab coat—gathered around a stone monument or statue base, handling a wooden structure, suggesting a historical ceremony or event. The next frame shows a formal portrait of Charles III in Hunting Dress (1786), an oil painting depicting the king in a gray hunting coat, white cravat, and tricorn hat, holding a rifle, set against a neutral background, reflecting his regal yet active persona. This is followed by a close-up of a figure from Goya’s *Self-Portrait at an Easel* (1790), showing the artist in a red dress and black hat, holding a paintbrush and palette, standing before an easel, representing his earlier, confident artistic phase. The sequence then shifts to a dark, expressive oil painting of two grotesque, emaciated figures in a close embrace, one with a long white beard and dark robes, the other a demonic-looking figure with a mouth agape in a silent scream, from the *Two Old Men Painting*. The video then cuts to a full view of *The Third of May 1808* (1814), a powerful oil painting by Goya depicting a man in a white shirt with arms raised before a firing squad, surrounded by fallen bodies under a dark sky, evoking horror and resistance. This is followed by a modern news broadcast with the chyron 'DEVELOPING NOW: CIVILIANS UNDER ATTACK', showing a fiery explosion and chaotic footage of people fleeing in rubble, creating a jarring contemporary parallel to the historical painting. The final frame is a close-up of *Saturn Devouring His Son* (1819–1823), a dark, visceral oil painting by Goya showing a gaunt, wild-eyed figure with long hair devouring a child, rendered in thick, expressive brushwork with stark contrasts of red and black, focusing on the face and the act of consumption, emphasizing themes of violence and despair.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a completely black screen, creating a moment of silence and anticipation. After a few seconds, white text fades in at the center of the screen, reading 'By James Payne' in a simple, serif font. The text remains static for several seconds, allowing the viewer to absorb the credit. Then, the text fades out, returning the screen to complete blackness. There are no other visual elements, movements, or changes in lighting throughout the segment. The atmosphere is minimalistic and formal, serving as an introductory title card or credit sequence. No other entities, people, or objects are present or interact within this minute.
